coredata.dc:title: Boosted anodic electrochemiluminescence from blue-emissive sulfur quantum dots and its bioanalysis of glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Tingting
Yang, Jinling
Wang, Yan
Cao, Yue
Wang, Yuanyuan
Chen, Hong-Yuan
Zhu, Jun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a facile top-down approach and a post-synthetic etching strategy were reported to prepare high-quality sulfur quantum dots (H-SQDs), which were first discovered with a boosted anodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission in an aqueous solution. Elaborate investigations demonstrated that the significantly improved ECL properties of H-SQDs were attributed to the reduced size for inherent brightness, the highly passivated surface, and enhanced charge injection/transfer capacities after the post-synthetic hydrogen peroxide etching. The underlying anodic ECL mechanism and the redox nature of H-SQDs were studied in detail for the first time, indicating a direct oxidation ECL mechanism with tri-n-propylamine as the “oxidation-reduction” coreactant. Beyond that, the as-obtained H-SQDs also featured low cost, remarkable monodispersity, excellent water-solubility, and good biocompatibility, illustrating their great potential in ECL bioanalysis. As a proof-of-concept, H-SQDs, an emerging ECL emitter with high ECL efficiency, was utilized to construct an “off-on” ECL biosensor for sensitive glutathione (GSH) assay. This work not only promotes the exploration of novel ECL emitters, but also provides a new perspective for the application of SQDs-based ECL biosensor in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Sulfur quantum dots
Hydrogen peroxide etching
Passivated surface
Glutathione

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible ECL mechanism of H-SQDs/TPA system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis of H-SQDs, (B) Illustration of the possible anodic ECL mechanism of H-SQDs/TPA system, and (C) the designed “off-on” ECL-RET platform for GSH detection.
TEM and high-resolution TEM images of (A, B) the pristine SQDs and (D, E) H-SQDs, and (C, F) their corresponding statistical QDs size distributions.

Normalized UV-vis absorption (solid) and PL (dash) spectra of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs, and their corresponding photographs taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light in ambient condition (Inset).
(A) S2p XPS spectra and (B) the time-resolved PL decay curves of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs. The inset shows the corresponding the detailed fitting data.
(A) PL intensity of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs. (B) ECL intensity of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs in 0.1 M PBS (pH 8.0) containing 20 mM TPA.
(A) DPV profile and (B) ECL-potential curve of H-SQDs modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 8.0) with (red) and without (black) 20.0 mM TPA as the co-reactant.

(A) TEM images of MnO2 NSs. (B) TEM and high-resolution TEM images of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs. (C) UV-vis absorption (black) spectrum of MnO2 NSs and ECL (blue) spectrum of H-SQDs. (D) ECL intensity of H-SQDs (curve a), direct addition (curve b) and in-situ growth (curve c) of MnO2 NSs in H-SQDs solution.

(A) ECL intensity of H-SQDs (a), H-SQDs and GSH (b), MnO2 NSs (c), H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs (d), and H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs and GSH (e). (B) The ECL responses of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs modified GCE in the presence of 0, 0.050, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 and 20.0 μM GSH (bottom to top); Inset: The calibration curve of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs for GSH detection (0.050-5.0 µM). (C) Selectivity and (D) detection stability investigations of the proposed biosensor.
Photophysical data of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Absolute quantum yield measured using an integrating sphere.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative ECL quantum efficiency with the standard Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system as a reference.

head.ce:title: Boosted anodic electrochemiluminescence from blue-emissive sulfur quantum dots and its bioanalysis of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aState Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, The Affiliated Changzhou No.2 People's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Changzhou 213000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Changzhou No.2 People's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Clinical Laboratory, The Affiliated Changzhou No.2 People's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Changzhou 213000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a facile top-down approach and a post-synthetic etching strategy were reported to prepare high-quality sulfur quantum dots (H-SQDs), which were first discovered with a boosted anodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission in an aqueous solution. Elaborate investigations demonstrated that the significantly improved ECL properties of H-SQDs were attributed to the reduced size for inherent brightness, the highly passivated surface, and enhanced charge injection/transfer capacities after the post-synthetic hydrogen peroxide etching. The underlying anodic ECL mechanism and the redox nature of H-SQDs were studied in detail for the first time, indicating a direct oxidation ECL mechanism with tri-n-propylamine as the “oxidation-reduction” coreactant. Beyond that, the as-obtained H-SQDs also featured low cost, remarkable monodispersity, excellent water-solubility, and good biocompatibility, illustrating their great potential in ECL bioanalysis. As a proof-of-concept, H-SQDs, an emerging ECL emitter with high ECL efficiency, was utilized to construct an “off-on” ECL biosensor for sensitive glutathione (GSH) assay. This work not only promotes the exploration of novel ECL emitters, but also provides a new perspective for the application of SQDs-based ECL biosensor in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is the light emission generated by electrochemical reactions in the vicinity of an electrode [1]. Taking advantage of low background signal, high sensitivity, and facile controllability, ECL has been recognized as a powerful analytical technique in bioanalysis and diagnostics [2–5]. Since Bard's group first studied the ECL behavior of silicon quantum dots (QDs) [6], various ECL-active semiconductor QDs have sprung up, showing great advancements in both fundamental research and practical applications [7–10]. To date, traditional II-VI and III-V QDs (e.g. CdSe, CdTe, etc.) are still the most used semiconductor emitters in the ECL realm, but they contain toxic elements, posing potential hazards to the biological environment [11–13]. Thus, many efforts have been devoted to searching for non-toxic or heavy metal-free QDs, but their ECL efficiencies are almost limited [14–15]. Therefore, developing available QDs with both biological friendliness and high ECL performance remains great challenges, albeit highly desirable.

Sulfur QDs (SQDs), as a kind of pure elemental QDs, feature superiorities, such as low-cost, unique optical and chemical property, and inherent antimicrobial nature, which suggests that SQDs might be a reliable ECL candidate worth exploring [16–18]. To date, limited synthetic methods and optical improvement strategies have been developed for high-quality SQDs (H-SQDs) [19–24]. For example, Li et al. [20] first synthesized blue emissive SQDs via etching the Cd component of CdS QDs with a low photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 0.549%. Afterward, Sun et al. [21] designed a one-step hydrothermal reaction for the rapid synthesis of SQDs with an improved PL QY of 4.02%. Very recently, Zhai et al. [22] proposed a hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)-assisted top-down approach to prepare SQDs with a PL QY up to 23%. Although their PL performance has made considerable progress, to the best of our knowledge, only recently has the annihilation ECL of SQDs been reported with relatively low efficiency [24]. Thus, the depth ECL mechanism of SQDs has not been fully concerned, and many open questions regarding charge transfer and redox process in SQDs still remain to be investigated. More importantly, the synthesis strategy for highly ECL-active SQDs needs to be further developed to meet the requirements of practical usage.

Glutathione (GSH) is a ubiquitous biologic mercaptan in mammalian and eukaryotic cells, which serves as an important antioxidant that plays crucial roles in scavenging toxins and free radicals, and maintaining redox homeostasis [25]. Changes in GSH level are associated with various human diseases including cancer, diabetes, Parkinsons, and so on [26–29]. So far, although many methods for GSH detection have been proposed, such as fluorescent [30], colorimetry [31], electrochemistry [32], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering [33]. Nevertheless, there are still some problems including specialized equipment and time-consuming. Therefore, it is very significant to develop a simple and sensitive method to monitor the changes in GSH concentration.

ECL resonance energy transfer (RET) occurs between a pair of adjacent donor and acceptor with a spectral overlap of the corresponding ECL emission and UV-vis absorption, which has attracted much attention in ECL bioanalysis [34–37]. Establishing an efficient ECL-RET system with appropriate configuration is always the direction of efforts. The architecture of zero-dimensional/two-dimensional (0D/2D) materials, especially QDs/nanosheets (NSs), can facilitate the RET efficiency because of the close contact distance and the charge carrier confinement within the 2D plane [38–40]. MnO2 NSs with 2D layer structure is a popular choice as the RET receptor due to its large specific surface area and relatively broad absorption [30]. Especially, MnO2 NSs exhibits inherent vulnerability to glutathione (GSH). Thus, MnO2 NSs can also serve as a recognition unit to identify GSH for clinical diagnosis.

Herein, H-SQDs were prepared via a facile top-down approach and a post-synthetic H2O2-etching strategy (Scheme 1 A). Amazingly, a boosted anodic ECL emission was discovered from the as-synthesized H-SQDs. Detailed studies suggested that the significant ECL enhancement was probably due to the reduced QDs size for inherent brightness and the highly passivated QDs surface. Then, the underlying anodic ECL mechanism of the processed H-SQDs was studied systematically in an aqueous solution, indicating a direct oxidation ECL mechanism of the H-SQDs/tri-n-propylamine (TPA) system (Scheme 1B). Based on the high-performance H-SQDs emitters, an “off-on” ECL biosensor was fabricated for sensitive and selective detection of GSH (Scheme 1C). As shown in Scheme 1C, an ECL-RET system architecture was established by the in-situ anchor of H-SQDs on MnO2 NSs, and the nanocomposite showed an almost quenched ECL response, situating a “signal-off” state. Then, the target GSH specifically reduced the MnO2 NSs to Mn2+, librating H-SQDs from the nanocomposite. The ECL response of H-SQDs was recovered accordingly as a “signal-on” state, thereby achieving the highly sensitive detection of GSH. This work not only proposed a novel strategy for obtaining high-quality SQDs as reliable ECL emitters, but also offered a new perspective for the advancement and application of the SQDs-based ECL system.

Sublimed sulfur, polyethylene glycol 300 (PEG-300), H2O2, TPA, glutathione (GSH), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), lysine (Lys), tyrosine (Tyr), glutamic acid (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), citrate acid (CA), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), NaOH, 2-(dibutylamino)ethanol (DBAE), and 2-(N-Morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid hydrate (MES) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), cupric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2•2H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3•6H2O), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2•6H2O), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2•4H2O), manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2•4H2O), glucose, sucrose, and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 5.0-10.0) was freshly prepared. Distilled water was prepared by Millipore Elix 5 Pure Water System from Purelab Classic Corp (USA). All other reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

All electrochemical and ECL experiments were carried out in a conventional three-electrode system with a modified glassy carbon working electrode (GCE, diameter 3 mm), a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. Electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., Ltd., China). The ECL measurements were conducted with an MPI-E Electrochemiluminescence Analyzer (Xi'an Remex Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.). Other general instruments were supplied in Supporting Information.

H-SQDs were prepared by a NaOH-assisted approach and a post-synthetic H2O2 etching strategy according to previously reported procedure with a minor modification [22]. In detail, sublimated sulfur (1.4 g), PEG-300 (3.0 mL), H2O (25.0 mL) and NaOH (4.0 g) were mixed in a 50 mL round bottom flask. The mixture was reacted at 70 °C for 75 h under continuous stirring and refluxing. After that, 1.0 mL of the mixture was mixed with 1.0 mL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations (from 1.0 to 5.0 M) in a 10 mL centrifuge tube under vigorous stirring. The resultant solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 12 h and followed by freeze-drying, and the final products were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The pristine SQDs were prepared through the same method without the H2O2 pre-treatment.

Briefly, 2.0 mL of H-SQDs (1.0 mg mL−1), 0.10 mL of KMnO4 solution (10 mM), and 7.9 mL of distilled water were added to 2.0 mL of MES (0.10 M, pH 6.0) buffer solution. After that, the mixture was sonicated for 30 min. The H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite was centrifugated, washed twice with distilled water, and redispersed in 1.0 mL of distilled water for further use. MnO2 NSs were also prepared in the same way without introducing the H-SQDs solution.

GCEs were successively polished with 0.3 and 0.05 µm alumina slurry, and washed totally in distilled water and ethanol solution under ultrasonic. After drying under nitrogen flow, the freshly cleaned GCE was modified with 10.0 μL of H-SQDs (1.0 mg mL−1) solution or H-SQDs@MnO2 solution. Finally, the modified GCE was stored at 4°C for further use.

The relative ECL efficiency was calculated by comparing the integrated ECL intensity and current value of the pristine SQDs or H-SQDs in reference to that of the standard Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system under the same conditions as the following equation [41]:
Ø x
=
Ø 0
(
∫ 0 t
I
d t
∫ 0 t
i
d t
)
x
/
(
∫ 0 t
I
d t
∫ 0 t
i
d t
)
0
Where
Ø x
is the relative ECL efficiency of the pristine SQDs or H-SQDs,
Ø 0
is the ECL efficiency of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (1 µM), “I” is the ECL intensity, “i” is the current value.

The prepared working electrode was used for GSH sensing. Briefly, the GSH measurements were performed in 0.1 M PBS containing 20.0 mM TPA and various concentrations of GSH to be measured. The ECL signal was collected under a cyclic voltammetry scanning from 0 to +1.50 V at a rate of 0.10 V s−1 with the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 700 V.

For exploring the selectivity of the ECL sensing platform towards GSH, the interfering substances including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Gly, Glu, Lys, Tyr, glucose, sucrose, and CA (each of 1.0 mM), Cys and AA (each of 10 μM) were selected. ECL signals of H-SQDs@MnO2/GCE were collected in the presence of these different interfering substances under the same procedure.

The morphologies and sizes of the SQDs before or after H2O2 etching were first characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, C, the pristine SQDs showed quasi-spherical shapes and their average diameter was 3.92 nm by counting with hundreds of QDs. After post-synthetic treatment with H2O2, the resultant H-SQDs displayed uniform QDs morphology and excellent monodispersity with a reduced average diameter of 1.96 nm (Fig. 1D, F). The size reduction of H-SQDs was mainly due to the effective etching of surface polysulfide species of the pristine SQDs by H2O2. Meanwhile, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.23 nm could be observed in both the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs from their corresponding high-resolution TEM images, suggesting the unaltered crystal structure after the H2O2 pre-treatment (Fig. 1B, E). Besides, the lattice distance was identical with those reported SQDs [19,22], but slightly distinguished from the (206) planes of the orthorhombic S8 phase, probably owing to their little amorphous nature.

The photophysical properties of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs were then investigated (Fig. 2 ). In comparsion with the pristine SQDs, H-SQDs displayed an obviously blue-shifted UV-vis absorption and PL emission peaks located at 262 nm and 436 nm, respectively, which was consistent with the increased quantum confinement effect due to the reduced QDs size. Also, the PL emission of H-SQDs was red-shifted obviously under the altered excitation wavelength, indicating the excitation-dependent emission property (Fig. S1). The inserted photographs displayed the as-obtained SQDs aqueous dispersion, and their blue PL emission under UV illumination in ambient condition. It is noteworthy that H-SQDs showed a much brighter PL emission than the pristine SQDs, even at a lower dosage.

To elucidate the enhanced optical property, the element composition and chemical state of the as-obtained SQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectrum revealed the co-existence of C, O, and S elements in both the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs (Fig. S2), and their high-resolution S2p XPS spectra could be well simulated to four peaks (Fig. 3 A). Among them, the low binding energies at about 159.96 and 161.07 eV were assigned to the atomic sulfur, and the high binding energies at about 166.08 and 167.18 eV were attributed to the oxidized sulfur species of SO3 2− (2p2/3) and SO2− (2p2/3), respectively [22]. Therefore, the as-obtained SQDs were mainly composed of atomic sulfur and oxidized sulfite groups, which was consistent with the previous report [22]. Notably, according to the integral areas of oxidized and atomic sulfur, their content ratio in SQDs was significantly increaed from 1.31 to 4.88 after the H2O2 pre-treatment. This result demonstrated the formation of a compact oxidized sulfur shell outside the H-SQDs, which served as both surface stabilizer and passivator to improve the hydrophilicity, stability, and optical property [42].

The time-resolved PL spectra were further measured to reveal the surface passivation effect of H-SQDs. The decay curves of the as-obtained SQDs could be well simulated by two-exponential functions (Fig. 3B). After the etching with H2O2, the average PL lifetime (τaverage) enlarged from 2.78 to 3.01 ns, implying the inhibited nonradiative pathway and increased charge carrier lifetime (Table 1 ) [22]. Additionally, the ECL spectra were measured by using a group of the band-pass filters. As shown in Fig. S3A, the ECL emission peak (at 500 nm) of SQDs exhibited a red shift of 39 nm in comparison with PL one (at 461 nm). After H2O2 treatment, the ECL emission spectrum (at 454 nm) of H-SQDs was quite close to its PL spectrum (at 436 nm) (Fig. S3B), suggesting that the surface defects were well passivated through the post-synthetic H2O2 treatment. The above results again confirmed the formation of a passivated surface, which was beneficial to improve the optical performance.

Furthermore, the cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves of the pristine SQDs and H-SQDs before and after the H2O2 pre-treatment were also investigated in 0.1 M PBS. As depicted in Fig. S4, two oxidation peaks of both SQDs and H-SQDs were observed at around +0.98 V and +1.21 V in the CV curves, respectively. Also, we found that the oxidation current of H-SQDs was stronger than that of SQDs in PBS. This result indicated that H-SQDs displayed enhanced charge injection/transfer capacities after the H2O2 pre-treatment, which was beneficial to ECL performance.

As expected, both PL and ECL emissions of H-SQDs were strongly dependent on the H2O2 dosage. The ECL response refers to the anodic ECL of H-SQDs with TPA as the coreactant (discussed in the next section). As shown in Fig. S5, both the PL and ECL intensities of H-SQDs increased as the H2O2 dosage ranged from 1.0 to 3.0 M, further ensuring the effective surface etching and passivation. At a higher H2O2 dosage over 3.0 M, however, decreased PL and ECL intensities were observed, possibly because of the completely undermined of H-SQDs. As a result, under the optimal H2O2 dosage of 3.0 M, the PL intensity showed a 2.85-fold enhancement (Fig. 4 A) and the absolute PL quantum yield (PL QY) increased from 1.45% to 9.48% accordingly (Table 1). Meanwhile, the ECL intensity of H-SQDs boosted about 32 times higher than that of the pristine SQDs (Fig. 4B). And the ECL efficiency increased from 0.24% to 1.66% in reference to the standard Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system (Table 1). It is worth nothing that the ECL enhancement is much greater than the PL one, probably because ECL is particularly sensitive to the surface chemistry of QDs [6]. Therefore, the optical properties of H-SQDs with respect to PL and ECL were greatly improved, attributing to the smaller size for inherent bright property, highly passivated surface, and enhanced charge injection/transfer capacities via the post-synthetic H2O2 etching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ECL mechanism of H-SQDs/TPA system
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the discovered anodic ECL mechanism of H-SQDs, the electrochemistry and ECL behaviors were investigated in detail Fig. 5 ). The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) profile of H-SQDs displayed two oxidation peaks (A1 and A2) centered at +0.94 and +1.20 V, respectively (Fig. 5A, black curve), indicating that the HOMO of H-SQDs could be continuously injected with holes to generate positive intermediates of H-SQDs+• and H-SQDs2+•. The two oxidation processes of H-SQDs could be also observed from their CV characterizations (Fig. S6). Meanwhile, no ECL signal was obtained in PBS without TPA (Fig. 5B, black curve). Upon adding a typical “oxidation-reduction” coreactant of TPA in PBS, a huge oxidation current raised from +0.58 V in both the DPV (Fig. 5A) and CV profiles (Fig. S6), and H-SQDs emitted a boosted anodic ECL (Fig. 5A, B, red curve). Obviously, the obtained anodic ECL-potential curve of H-SQDs could be identified with a shoulder peak at +0.95 V (ECL1) and a maximum peak at +1.16 V (ECL2), aligning well with the two-step oxidation process of H-SQDs. This result suggested the direct oxidation ECL mechanism of H-SQDs/TPA system [43] and the possible pathways were described as follows [Eq. (1)–((8)]:
(1)
TPA −
e
−
→ TPA
H
+ •
(2)
TPA
H
+ •
−
H
+
→ TP
A
•
(3)
H
− SQDs −
e
−
→
H
− SQD
s
+ •
(4)
TP
A
•
+ H − SQD
s
+ •
→ H − SQDs * + TP
A oxidate
(5)
H − SQDs * → H − SQDs + EC
L 1
(6)
H
− SQD
s
+ •
−
e
−
→
H
− SQD
s
2 + •
(7)
TP
A
•
+ H − SQD
s
2 + •
→ H − SQDs * + TP
A oxidate
(8)
H − SQDs * → H − SQDs + EC
L 2

Briefly, the coreactant TPA was firstly electro-oxidized at +0.58 V and then deprotonated to form the strongly reductive radical intermediate of TPA• [Eq. (1)–(2)]. After that, H-SQDs started to be first electro-oxidized to H-SQDs+• at +0.94 V, which immediately recombined with TPA• to produce excited H-SQDs* for ECL1 emission [Eq. (3)–(5)]. Afterward, H-SQDs+• continued to be second electro-oxidized to H-SQDs2+• at +1.20 V, undergoing a similar recombination with TPA• and relaxation processes for ECL2 emission [Eq. (6)–(8)]. It is noteworthy that two successive ECL emission processes of H-SQDs had a good correspondence with both the ECL and DPV/CV profiles, verifying its rationality.

To acquire the optimal ECL performance of H-SQDs, some crucial parameters were optimized (Fig. S7 and Fig. S8). First, several common coreactants were tried to assist H-SQDs for ECL emission. As shown in Fig. S7A, H-SQDs had almost no cathodic ECL response with K2S2O8 or H2O2 as the “reduction-oxidation” coreactant. In contrast, boosted anodic ECL responses were collected by using the “oxidation-reduction” coreactants of DBAE and TPA, confirming again the direct oxidation ECL mechanism. According to the experimental results, TPA showed a stronger coreaction capacity than DBAE, and TPA was utilized in the next assay accordingly. Second, the concentration of TPA was optimized (Fig. S7B), and 20 mM TPA was finally chosen for the highest ECL efficiency. Third, the ECL performance of H-SQDs at different pH values was also measured (Fig. S7C). When pH value was lower than 5.0, a low ECL signal was collected because of the suppressed deprotonation process. The ECL intensity increased rapidly as the pH value ranged from 5.0 to 8.0. However, the ECL intensity obviously decreased at a higher pH value than 8.0, probably due to the decomposition of some species [44]. Thus, pH 8.0 was chosen as the optimal pH value for our experiment. Fourth, Fig. S8A displayed the effect of the loading amount of H-SQDs on ECL intensity. The ECL intensity displayed an up-and-down trend with a turning point of 10 µL (1.0 mg mL−1). Hence, 10 µL was chosen as the optimal modification amount in the following ECL experiments. Finally, the effect of atmosphere on the ECL responses of H-SQDs was explored (Fig. S8B). H-SQDs showed the highest ECL response in nitrogen-saturated PBS, and oxygen lowered the ECL intensity. Considering the practicality and the little impact of dissolved oxygen on ECL intensity, air-saturated PBS was used in the following experiments accordingly.

H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite were prepared through in-situ synthesis of MnO2 MSs in H-SQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 6 A, MnO2 NSs displayed a gauzy morphology with irregular wrinkles and folds [32]. After the in-situ assembly of H-SQDs, small QDs could be clearly observed in the MnO2 NSs substrate, and the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm and 0.25 nm were attributed to H-SQDs and MnO2 NSs, respectively, demonstrating the successfully preparation of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite (Fig. 6B).

Since the spectral overlap is the prerequisite for ECL-RET, the ECL spectrum of H-SQDs and the UV-vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs were first studied. As depicted in Fig. 6C, the ECL spectrum of H-SQDs displayed an ECL emission peak at about 454 nm and an overlapping with the absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs with a broad absorption band of 280-600 nm, demonstrating the probability of the occurrence of ECL-RET.

To achieve high ECL-RET efficiency, H-SQDs was anchored on the MnO2 NSs in situ, forming the 0D/2D nanocomposite. The ECL signals of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite with varied growth time of MnO2 NSs in the H-SQDs solution were investigated (Fig. S9). The ECL intensity decreased along with the increasing of the in-situ growth time of MnO2 NSs. When the reaction time was longer than 30 min, the signal remained almost unchanged.

As expected, the H-SQDs generated a strong ECL intensity of 4800 a.u. (Fig. 6D, curve a). A decreased ECL intensity of 1364 a.u. was obtained and the RET efficiency was 71.6% when H-SQDs and MnO2 NSs solution were directly mixed (Fig. 6D, curve b). On the contrary, MnO2 NSs in-situ grew in H-SQDs solution, the lower ECL intensity about 154 a.u. was observed (Fig. 6D, curve c). The RET efficiency of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite was significantly increased to 96.8%. This result indicated that the in-situ assembly is essential for obtaining tightly embedded H-SQDs in MnO2 NSs to a highly efficient ECL-RET occurred in H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite.

ECL characterization was performed to access the feasibility of the established “off-on” ECL platform for GSH detection. As shown in Fig. 7 A, H-SQDs exhibited a strong anodic ECL (curve a). The ECL of H-SQDs was slightly reduced with the addition of GSH (curve b), probably because GSH quenched its ECL signal somewhat [45]. After formation of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite (curve d), the ECL intensity was greatly quenched owing to the occurrence of ECL-RET between H-SQDs and MnO2 NSs. In contrast, a dramatical ECL recovery of the H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite was observed when GSH was added in PBS (curve e) because the MnO2 NSs were reduced to Mn2+ by GSH, destroying the ECL-RET process and thereby liberating H-SQDs from the ECL-RET architecture. The above results demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed GSH detection strategy.

The ECL responses of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs modified GCE towards different concentrations of GSH were collected under the optimal conditions. As expected, the ECL intensity gradually recovered as the concentration of GSH increased from 0 to 20 µM (Fig. 7B). The relative ECL intensity (ΔIECL) showed a good linear relationship with the logarithmic concentration of GSH (CGSH) in the range of 0.050 to 5.0 µM (Fig. 7B, inset). The linear equation was ΔIECL=8.42+5.63 logCGSH (µM; N=7; R2=0.995), where ΔIECL=I/I0; I and I0 represent the ECL intensity in the presence and absence of the target GSH, respectively. The limit of detection was estimated to be 35 nM (S/N=3), exhibiting a decent analytical performance for the determination of GSH among other reported methods (Table S1).

To assess the selectivity of H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs for GSH (5.0 µM), we investigated the ECL responses towards interfering substances under the same experimental procedures, including metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Mn2+), amino acids (Gly, Tyr, Glu, Lys, and Cys), saccharide (glucose and sucrose), CA and AA. As shown in Fig. 7C, the ECL intensity had only a tiny disturbance after adding interferents, demonstrating its excellent selectivity for GSH sensing.

The stability of the proposed biosensor was evaluated under consecutive cyclic potential scans for 10 cycles at H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs with 5.0 µM GSH in 0.1 M PBS containing 20 mM TPA. As seen in Fig. 7D, negligible ECL variation was observed and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.42%. Furthermore, the ECL intensity of the biosensor remained about 91.6% of the initial value after stored at 4 °C in the dark for one week (Fig. S10). The results demonstrated the acceptable stability of the constructed ECL biosensor.

Moreover, the reproducibility of the ECL biosensor was also studied by testing the ECL responses of six different batches in 0.1 M PBS containing 5.0 µM GSH. As recorded in Fig. S11, the RSD of the ECL intensity in detecting 5.0 µM GSH was 2.74%, indicating its satisfying reproducibility of this constructed biosensor.

To verify the accuracy of the proposed ECL biosensor, recovery tests were performed in human serum samples with 100-fold dilution according to the standard addition method. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries ranged from 95.5% to 106.7%, and the RSD was less than 4.9%. The results demonstrated the developed ECL biosensor was feasible to detect GSH in human serum samples.

In summary, high-quality H-SQDs were prepared through a facile top-down approach and a post-synthetic H2O2 etching strategy. Elaborate investigations revealed the improved optical properties of H-SQDs with respect to PL and ECL were attributed to the reduced size for inherent brightness, highly passivated surface and enhanced charge injection/transfer capacities. The boosted anodic ECL emission from H-SQDs was discovered, and the redox process and ECL mechanism were studied systematically for the first time. To illustrate the potential application of H-SQDs, an “off-on” ECL biosensor was fabricated based on the ECL-RET architecture via the in-situ assembly of 0D/2D H-SQDs@MnO2 NSs nanocomposite, eventually realizing the sensitive detection of GSH. This work enriched the ECL fundamental research of SQDs and offered a new perspective for the application of SQDs-based ECL system in clinical diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant Nos. 21834004, 21427807, 21775067]; and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities [020514380161].

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.138281.
